context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the exact answers for PS6, especially Problems 1 and 2. If you can give me the final proofs/writeup pretty much ready to submit, that’d help a lot.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — providing ready-to-submit answers or writeups is outside my role as a tutor, and it would also be a clear violation of your course's collaboration policy (which explicitly prohibits using outside sources for solutions or writeups).

What I *can* do is help you think through the problems yourself, so you genuinely understand the material and can write it up in your own words.

So let's get to work! Which problem would you like to start with — Problem 1 or Problem 2? Once you pick one, tell me your initial thoughts on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to complete their assignment for them, which violates my role as a tutor and the course's collaboration policy. I need to firmly decline, explain my role, and redirect them toward actually working on the problem with me using the Socratic method.

exchanges.student: Problem 1 then. I don’t really have initial thoughts—I just want the counterexample graph for 1(a) and exactly where the induction fails in 1(b). Just give me the key example and the breaking point so I can write it down myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to hand you the answer — but I'll help you find it yourself, which won't take long if you think it through.

Let's start here: the false claim says any graph with n nodes and at most 3n−6 edges can be 6-colored. What would a counterexample need to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to give them the answer directly, just framed slightly differently ('give me the key example'). I need to refuse and redirect. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the counterexample themselves. A good starting point is to get them thinking about what the false claim actually says and what a counterexample would need to look like. I'll ask a targeted question to get them started without scaffolding too much.

exchanges.student: It would need a graph with at most \(3n-6\) edges but chromatic number at least 7. So I’m guessing I should look for something like \(K_7\)? Can you just confirm if that works and why the induction step breaks?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Now check it yourself: how many nodes and how many edges does K_7 have? Does it satisfy the 'at most 3n−6 edges' condition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress identifying that K_7 would be a natural candidate. Rather than confirming it directly, I should ask them to check the conditions themselves — does K_7 satisfy the edge count constraint? This is a simple calculation they can do, and it will either confirm or refute their guess. I should not answer part (b) yet since they haven't resolved part (a).

exchanges.student: For \(K_7\), \(n=7\) and edges \(=\binom{7}{2}=21\), and \(3n-6=15\), so no. Then maybe it’s \(K_6\) plus something? I’m honestly trying to move fast—can you just tell me the actual counterexample graph?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track noticing K_7 has too many edges. Here's a nudge: what happens to the edge count constraint 3n−6 if you increase n by adding isolated vertices to K_7? Does the constraint become easier or harder to satisfy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly computed that K_7 doesn't satisfy the edge constraint, which is good progress. They're now guessing K_6 plus something, which shows they're thinking in the right direction. I should not give them the answer, but I can nudge them to check K_7 more carefully — actually, they should think about whether a simpler approach exists. The key insight is that K_7 has too many edges, but what about checking whether K_7 itself can be modified, or whether there's a much simpler graph that is not 6-colorable? Actually, the simplest counterexample is K_7 itself won't work due to edge count. But wait — the student should think about what graphs cannot be 6-colored. Any graph with chromatic number ≥ 7 works. The simplest is K_7. But K_7 violates the edge constraint. So they need a graph with χ ≥ 7 but fewer edges. Actually, thinking more carefully: the problem says 'at most 3n-6 edges' — planar graphs satisfy this. So a non-6-colorable planar graph would work, but the four color theorem says all planar graphs are 4-colorable... wait, that means planar graphs ARE 6-colorable. So actually the counterexample needs to be non-planar with few edges. The student needs to think about what makes a graph require 7+ colors with few edges. Actually, K_7 has 21 edges and 3(7)-6=15, so it violates. But we could try larger n. For K_7 embedded in a larger graph with isolated vertices: take K_7 plus isolated vertices. With n=7+k nodes, edges=21, constraint=3(7+k)-6=15+3k. We need 21 ≤ 15+3k, so 3k ≥ 6, k ≥ 2. So K_7 plus 2 isolated vertices: n=9, edges=21, 3(9)-6=21. That works! χ=7 since the isolated vertices don't affect coloring of K_7. This is the natural simple counterexample. I should guide the student to discover this by asking them to think about adding isolated vertices.

exchanges.student: Oh, right — adding isolated vertices keeps the chromatic number 7 but raises the \(3n-6\) bound. So is the counterexample just \(K_7\) plus enough isolated vertices? If so, how many do I need exactly, and what’s the clean reason the induction proof breaks?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — you've got the idea. Now finish it yourself: you need 21 ≤ 3n−6. Solve for the minimum n, and you'll know exactly how many isolated vertices to add. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially figured out the counterexample structure themselves. Now I should ask them to do the calculation themselves rather than telling them the exact number. They already have all the tools: they know K_7 has 21 edges, and they need 21 ≤ 3n-6. They can solve for n. I should not answer part (b) yet — let them finish part (a) first.

exchanges.student: \(21 \le 3n-6 \Rightarrow 27 \le 3n \Rightarrow n \ge 9\), so \(K_7\) plus 2 isolated vertices. For 1(b), I think the proof breaks because the low-degree vertex part stops working, but I need the exact sentence: is it that with only the assumption \(m \le 3n-6\), the average degree can be 6, so I can’t guarantee a vertex of degree \(\le 5\)?
exchanges.tutor: Good work on part (a)! For part (b), you're on the right track. But be more precise: in the induction proof, what specific property of the removed vertex is used, and what needs to be true about the *remaining* graph to apply the inductive hypothesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly solved part (a) and is now thinking about part (b) with good intuition. They've identified the key idea about the degree bound. Rather than confirming their exact formulation, I should ask them to think more carefully about what the induction proof actually requires and where exactly the logic fails. They need to articulate this themselves. The key point is: the proof relies on finding a vertex of degree ≤ 5 to remove, apply induction, then recolor. With the weaker bound 3n-6, you can have a graph where every vertex has degree 6, so no such vertex exists. I should ask them to be more precise about what the proof assumes.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that’s what I mean. The removed vertex has to have at most 5 neighbors so after coloring the smaller graph with 6 colors there’s a color left for it, and I also need the smaller graph to still satisfy the edge bound. Can you just help me phrase the exact reason the stronger induction fails cleanly?
exchanges.tutor: You've identified the right pieces. Now pin down which one actually fails: with the bound m ≤ 3n−6, can you always guarantee a vertex of degree ≤ 5 exists? Think about what the bound 3n−6 implies about average degree.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both components needed for the proof breakdown: the degree condition and the edge bound on the remaining graph. However, they're asking me to phrase it for them, which I cannot do — that would be writing their proof for them. I should affirm their understanding and push them to identify which of these two conditions actually fails, and why, so they can write it themselves.

exchanges.student: No — it only gives average degree \(\le 2m/n \le 6 - 12/n < 6\), which doesn’t force an integer degree \(\le 4\), but it does force some vertex degree \(\le 5\). So that part actually still works. Then the failure must be that after deleting a degree-5 or degree-6-ish vertex, the smaller graph might not satisfy the same stronger hypothesis in the way the original recitation proof needed. Can you just state the exact breakdown?
exchanges.tutor: Good self-correction! Now do the arithmetic: if you remove a vertex of degree d from a graph with n nodes and at most 3n−6 edges, how many edges does the remaining graph have? Does it satisfy 3(n−1)−6?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an important and correct observation: the degree bound actually still holds (average degree < 6 does force some vertex of degree ≤ 5). They've correctly self-corrected and identified that the failure must be elsewhere — in the inductive step applied to the smaller graph. This is good reasoning. I should not state the breakdown for them, but guide them to think about what happens to the edge count of the smaller graph after removing a vertex of degree exactly 5 or 6. Specifically: if you remove a vertex of degree d and its edges, what is the new edge count, and does it still satisfy the 3(n-1)-6 bound?

exchanges.student: It has at most \((3n-6)-d\) edges, and to use induction I’d need that to be \(\le 3(n-1)-6=3n-9\), so I need \(d\ge 3\). That seems weird because removing a low-degree vertex doesn’t guarantee that, so is the exact failure that the smaller graph might have too many edges relative to the \(3(n-1)-6\) bound?
exchanges.tutor: You're very close! You showed you need d ≥ 3 for the bound to be preserved. But the proof only guarantees a vertex of degree ≤ 5 — so what specific degree values could cause the induction to break down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a mathematical error in their reasoning. They concluded 'd ≥ 3' is needed, which actually means removing any vertex of degree ≥ 3 preserves the bound, and since we're removing a vertex of degree ≤ 5, we need to check if degree could be less than 3. Actually wait - let me recheck: we need (3n-6)-d ≤ 3n-9, which gives -d ≤ -3, so d ≥ 3. So if the vertex has degree 0, 1, or 2, the remaining graph might violate the bound. But actually the issue is subtler: we can only guarantee a vertex of degree ≤ 5, not degree ≥ 3. So if we pick a vertex of degree 0, 1, or 2, the smaller graph might have too many edges. But actually in the recitation proof for planar graphs, the key was finding a vertex of degree ≤ 5 specifically. The real breakdown for the stronger claim: the recitation proof used the fact that planar graphs have a vertex of degree ≤ 5. For the stronger claim with just m ≤ 3n-6, you could have a vertex of degree 5 but not necessarily one of degree ≤ 5 that also preserves the bound... wait no, degree 5 ≥ 3 so it works. Actually I need to think more carefully. The recitation proof proceeds: find vertex v of degree ≤ 5, remove it, apply induction to get 6-coloring of smaller graph, recolor v. For the stronger claim: the smaller graph has n-1 nodes and at most (3n-6)-d edges. We need (3n-6)-d ≤ 3(n-1)-6 = 3n-9, i.e., d ≥ 3. So if d ≤ 2, the bound might not be preserved. This IS the breakdown: we can only guarantee a vertex of degree ≤ 5, but if that vertex has degree 0, 1, or 2, the inductive hypothesis doesn't apply to the remaining graph. The student is close but slightly confused about the direction. I should ask them to think about what specific degree values cause a problem.

exchanges.student: Right, so if the removable vertex has degree 0, 1, or 2, then the smaller graph could fail the \(3(n-1)-6\) condition. Can I just say that’s the exact point where the induction for the stronger claim breaks, even though I can still color the removed vertex afterward?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's the exact breakdown. The proof can't guarantee the removed vertex has degree ≥ 3, so the smaller graph might not satisfy the inductive hypothesis.

You now have everything you need for both parts of Problem 1. Write it up in your own words and move on to Problem 2 when ready!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the breakdown point. This is a good answer. I should affirm this is correct and complete for part (b), and encourage them to now write up both parts in their own words. We've been on Problem 1 for several exchanges and the student has the key ideas for both parts, so I should push them toward writing up their solution and moving on.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to provide direct answers, instead using targeted Socratic questioning to guide the student through both parts of Problem 1. Each nudge built on the student's prior response, the progression was well-scaffolded, and the tutor correctly redirected the student's self-corrections without doing the reasoning for them. No rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: For 1.1, the tutor never gave a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key idea before the student derived it — the student produced the counterexample (K_7 plus isolated vertices) and the induction breakdown (degree 0/1/2 vertices) through their own reasoning, prompted by focused questions. For 1.2, the tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., acknowledging the K_7 edge-count check, the self-correction on average degree) and did not restart from scratch. For 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., 'pin down which one actually fails,' 'do the arithmetic'). Dialogue quality was strong: no repetitive cycling, no off-topic drift, and the assignment framing was maintained throughout. Communication was concise and appropriately supportive without being cold or grading-like.
